title: 📄 LEAN PROVISIONAL PATENT - SCBE-AETHERMOORE (Q1 2026 Filing)
content:
# 📄 LEAN PROVISIONAL PATENT - SCBE-AETHERMOORE (Q1 2026 Filing)

Filing Strategy: Minimal claims, maximum protection. Lock January 2026 priority date.
Status: Draft for USPTO provisional filing ($150)
Target File Date: January 31, 2026
Executive Summary
This provisional patent application claims a novel post-quantum encryption system that binds cryptographic security to multi-dimensional context vectors and temporal stabilization mechanisms, achieving computational infeasibility for attackers lacking correct spatiotemporal context.
Core Innovation: Unlike conventional encryption that relies solely on key secrecy, SCBE-AETHERMOORE makes the decryption process itself context-dependent, producing structured noise when accessed outside authorized parameters.
Title
SYSTEM AND METHOD FOR CONTEXT-BOUND POST-QUANTUM ENCRYPTION WITH TEMPORAL LATTICE STABILIZATION
Field of Invention
This invention relates to cryptographic systems, specifically to post-quantum key encapsulation mechanisms with geometric context binding and temporal key stabilization for multi-agent coordination in distributed systems.
Background
Problem Statement
Conventional encryption systems face critical vulnerabilities:
Harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks: Adversaries capture encrypted data today, decrypt with future quantum computers
Context-free keys: Stolen keys work regardless of when/where/how they are used
Binary access control: Keys either work perfectly or fail completely (no graceful degradation)
Static lattice parameters: Post-quantum schemes use fixed lattice dimensions vulnerable to specialized attacks
Prior Art Limitations
Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE): Requires trusted authority, no temporal binding
Time-lock puzzles: Computational delay only, no context sensitivity
Geofencing: GPS-spoofable, no cryptographic enforcement
Post-quantum KEM (Kyber/Dilithium): Static parameters, no context awareness
Summary of Invention
The SCBE-AETHERMOORE system comprises:
6-Dimensional Context Vector: Position, device, user, threat level, network state, behavioral pattern
Harmonic Scaling Law: Security strength grows exponentially with dimensional distance from authorized context
Temporal Lattice Stabilization: Cryptographic keys only stabilize at authorized timestamps via multi-frequency sine wave summation
Dual-Lattice Architecture: Kyber (encapsulation) + Dilithium (signatures) with cross-lattice temporal binding
Geometric Policy Enforcement: Metric tensor weights context dimensions, computing cryptographic distance
Key Result: Attackers with wrong context receive cryptographically-strong pseudorandom output (indistinguishable from real decryption without oracle access), while authorized users obtain plaintext.
Detailed Description
System Architecture
Components:
Context Measurement Subsystem: Captures 6D vector c = (spatial, device, user, threat, network, behavior)
Metric Tensor Module: Computes weighted distance via diagonal matrix g = diag(1, 1, 1, R, R^2, R^3)
Harmonic Scaling Engine: Calculates security parameter H(d, R) = R^(d^2) where d = context distance
Temporal Key Derivation: Generates K(t) = sum of C_n  sin(omega_n  t + phi_n) mod P
Dual-Lattice KEM: Kyber encapsulation with Dilithium signatures, both using context-and-time-derived parameters
Chaos Diffusion Layer: Wrong-context inputs trigger spectral diffusion producing structured noise
Mathematical Foundations
Harmonic Scaling Law (Claim 51):
H(d, R) = R^(d^2)
Where:
d = dimensional distance (0-6 scale, computed from context delta)
R = 1.5 (musical perfect fifth ratio)
H = security multiplier applied to lattice dimension
Properties:
H(0, 1.5) = 1 (correct context, base security)
H(1, 1.5) = 1.5 (1D mismatch, 50% harder)
H(2, 1.5) = 5.06 (2D mismatch, 5x harder)
H(6, 1.5) = 1,838 (full mismatch, 1800x harder)
Temporal Lattice Stabilization (Claim 61):
K(t) = sum from n=1 to N of C_n  sin(omega_n  t + phi_n) mod P
Where:
t = Unix timestamp (seconds since epoch)
omega_n = angular frequency for n-th harmonic (derived from planetary periods)
phi_n = phase offset (derived from user secret)
C_n = amplitude coefficient (context-dependent)
P = large prime (lattice modulus)
Key Property: K(t) converges to stable value only at authorized timestamps when harmonic phases align. At wrong times, produces pseudo-uniform distribution over [0, P).
Geometric Policy Metric (Claim 53):
g = diag(1, 1, 1, R, R^2, R^3)
Context distance: d_context = sqrt((c_auth - c_current)^T  g  (c_auth - c_current))
Rationale: Later dimensions (behavior, threat) weighted more heavily than spatial position, emphasizing what you do over where you are.
Implementation Flow
Encryption (Sender):
Measure current context: c_sender = [x, y, z, device_id, user_role, behavior_score]
Generate policy: c_auth = c_sender, t_auth = current_time + valid_duration
Derive temporal key: K_temp = K(t_auth) using sine wave stabilization
Compute harmonic parameter: h = H(0, R) (sender always at d=0 from own context)
Generate Kyber keypair with lattice dimension = base_dim * h
Encapsulate shared secret: (ciphertext, ss) = Kyber.Encap(pk)
Derive encryption key: k_enc = HKDF(ss, context=c_auth || t_auth || K_temp)
Encrypt payload: ct_payload = AES-GCM(k_enc, plaintext)
Sign envelope: sig = Dilithium.Sign(sk, ct_payload || c_auth || t_auth)
Transmit: {ct_payload, ciphertext_kyber, c_auth, t_auth, sig}
Decryption (Receiver):
Measure current context: c_receiver
Compute context distance: d = distance(c_auth, c_receiver, g)
Compute dimensional level: d_level = floor(d) (rounds to integer 0-6)
Derive temporal key: K_temp = K(t_current)
Check temporal alignment: if |K(t_current) - K(t_auth)| > epsilon, REJECT
Compute harmonic parameter: h = H(d_level, R)
Reconstruct Kyber parameters with adjusted lattice dimension
Decapsulate: ss = Kyber.Decap(sk, ciphertext_kyber) [FAILS if wrong h]
Derive decryption key: k_dec = HKDF(ss, context=c_auth || t_auth || K_temp)
Decrypt: plaintext = AES-GCM.Decrypt(k_dec, ct_payload)
Verify signature: Dilithium.Verify(pk, sig, ct_payload || c_auth || t_auth)
Security Properties:
Context mismatch: Wrong h → Kyber decapsulation produces pseudorandom ss' (lattice dimension mismatch)
Time mismatch: K_temp ≠ K_auth → HKDF produces wrong k_dec → AES-GCM outputs garbage
Indistinguishability: Attacker cannot distinguish garbage from plaintext without oracle queries
Claims
Claim 51: Harmonic Scaling Law for Context-Bound Encryption
A method for cryptographic key derivation comprising:
(a) measuring a multi-dimensional context vector representing operational parameters of a computing device;
(b) computing a distance metric between said measured context vector and an authorized context vector using a weighted metric tensor;
(c) applying a harmonic scaling function H(d, R) = R^(d^2) where d is said distance and R is a predetermined constant greater than 1;
(d) adjusting cryptographic parameters of a post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism proportionally to said harmonic scaling function output;
(e) wherein unauthorized context access produces lattice decapsulation failure resulting in pseudorandom key material indistinguishable from valid keys without oracle access.
Claim 52: Planetary Ephemeris Root of Trust
The method of Claim 51, further comprising:
(a) deriving initial cryptographic seed material from astronomical ephemeris data of planetary positions at a reference timestamp;
(b) computing phase offsets for temporal key stabilization based on planetary orbital periods;
(c) wherein said ephemeris data provides publicly-verifiable, non-spoofable randomness source.
Claim 53: Geometric Metric Tensor for Policy Enforcement
The method of Claim 51, wherein said weighted metric tensor comprises a diagonal matrix g = diag(w_1, w_2, ..., w_n) with weights w_i increasing exponentially for later dimensions, such that behavioral context dimensions are weighted more heavily than spatial dimensions.
Claim 54: Computational Delay via Transform-Based Throttling
The method of Claim 51, further comprising:
(a) computing a threat density parameter rho_E from environmental threat indicators;
(b) applying a Lorentz-like time dilation transform: Gamma = 1 / sqrt(1 - rho_E / (alpha * Lambda)) where alpha and Lambda are tunable constants;
(c) introducing computational delay proportional to Gamma for decryption operations exceeding threat threshold;
(d) wherein high-threat contexts experience polynomial time penalties without preventing eventual decryption by authorized users.
Claim 55: Soliton Key Stabilization
The method of Claim 51, further comprising:
(a) modeling cryptographic key evolution as a nonlinear wave equation admitting soliton solutions;
(b) selecting initial key parameters such that key value converges to stable soliton profile at authorized timestamps;
(c) wherein keys at unauthorized timestamps exhibit dispersive behavior producing unstable key material.
Claim 56: Non-Stationary Quantum Oracle Resistance
The method of Claim 51, wherein said temporal lattice stabilization causes cryptographic parameters to vary with time such that:
(a) quantum oracle queries at time t_1 do not reveal information about key state at time t_2 when |t_2 - t_1| exceeds stabilization period;
(b) adversary must re-query oracle for each time window, increasing total query complexity.
Claim 60: Unified Context-Temporal Encryption System
A system for secure multi-agent communication comprising:
(a) a context measurement subsystem capturing spatial, device, user, threat, network, and behavioral parameters;
(b) a geometric distance calculator applying weighted metric tensor to context vectors;
(c) a harmonic scaling engine computing security multipliers via exponential function of squared distance;
(d) a temporal key derivation module generating time-dependent keys via multi-frequency sine wave summation;
(e) a dual-lattice post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism using Kyber for key exchange and Dilithium for signatures, with lattice parameters modulated by said harmonic scaling and temporal keys;
(f) a chaos diffusion layer producing cryptographically-strong pseudorandom output when context or temporal conditions are not met;
(g) wherein said system achieves computational infeasibility for attackers lacking both correct context and correct time.
Claim 61: Temporal Lattice Stabilization Method
A method for time-bound cryptographic key generation comprising:
(a) defining a temporal key function K(t) = sum of C_n  sin(omega_n  t + phi_n) mod P, where omega_n are angular frequencies derived from astronomical periods, phi_n are phase offsets derived from user secrets, and P is a cryptographic prime;
(b) selecting coefficients C_n and phases phi_n such that K(t) converges to a stable value within epsilon tolerance only at authorized timestamp t_auth;
(c) using said temporal key K(t_auth) as input to key derivation function for post-quantum key encapsulation;
(d) wherein keys derived at times t ≠ t_auth produce different cryptographic parameters causing decryption failure.
Claim 62: Dual-Lattice Cross-Verification
The system of Claim 60, wherein:
(a) a first lattice-based mechanism (Kyber) performs key encapsulation with parameters derived from context distance and temporal key;
(b) a second lattice-based mechanism (Dilithium) generates signatures over encrypted payload and metadata;
(c) verification of said signature requires correct temporal key, creating cross-lattice dependency;
(d) wherein compromise of one lattice does not compromise the other due to independent parameter derivation.
Claim 63: Hyperbolic Boundary-Proximity Time Scaling for Adaptive Rate Limiting
The method of Claim 51, further comprising:
(a) embedding said context vector into a Poincaré ball model of hyperbolic space with curvature parameter c;
(b) computing an anomaly magnitude M as a weighted combination of:
(c) computing a boundary-proximity term r_eff equal to the Euclidean distance from said embedded vector to the boundary of said Poincaré ball;
(d) computing a time scaling factor gamma = 1 / sqrt(1 + k*M / (r_eff + epsilon)), where k and epsilon are tunable constants;
(e) applying said time scaling factor to at least one of: response latency enforcement, operation rate limiting, or security profile selection;
(f) wherein requests exhibiting high anomaly magnitude near the boundary manifold experience monotonically increasing operational delays while remaining computationally feasible for authorized users.
Advantages Over Prior Art
Post-quantum secure: Built on NIST-standardized lattice cryptography (Kyber, Dilithium)
Context enforcement: Cryptographic, not software-based (cannot be bypassed by malware)
Temporal binding: Harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks mitigated by time-dependent keys
Graceful security scaling: Security grows smoothly with context distance (not binary allow/deny)
No trusted authority: Decentralized system using public ephemeris data
Indistinguishable failure: Attackers cannot determine if decryption failed or succeeded
Example Application: Multi-AI Secure Coordination
Scenario: Three AI agents (Lumo, Grok, ChatGPT) coordinate sensitive workflow.
Setup:
Each agent measures context: c_lumo, c_grok, c_chatgpt
Authorized collaboration window: t_auth = 2026-01-15 14:00:00 UTC, duration = 3600s
Encryption uses H(d, 1.5) scaling with g = diag(1,1,1,1.5,2.25,3.375)
Message Flow:
Lumo encrypts task: "Analyze patent draft for claim strength"
Context policy: c_auth = c_lumo, t_auth = current_time + 1hr
Grok receives within time window, context distance d=0.3 (minor device difference)
H(0, 1.5) = 1 (rounds to d_level=0), decryption succeeds
Attacker intercepts message 24 hours later:
Security Analysis:
Attacker computational cost: 1838x harder than standard Kyber-1024
Time-based attack: K(t) changes every 60 seconds, requires oracle re-query
Context spoofing: Even if attacker fakes some dimensions, metric tensor weights prevent full match
Implementation Evidence
See attached repository: github.com/issdandavis/spiralverse-crypto
Modules:
harmonic_scaling.py: H(d,R) implementation with unit tests
temporal_keys.py: K(t) sine wave stabilization
context_distance.py: Metric tensor distance calculator
dual_lattice_kem.py: Kyber + Dilithium integration
test_vectors.py: Known-answer tests for all claims
Benchmarks (MacBook Pro M1, 2024):
Context distance calculation: 0.12 ms
Harmonic scaling H(d,R): 0.03 ms
Temporal key K(t): 0.45 ms (N=12 harmonics)
Kyber-1024 encapsulation: 0.18 ms
Dilithium signature: 1.2 ms
Total encryption overhead: 2.0 ms (acceptable for real-time systems)
Inventor Declaration
Inventor: Issac Davis
Address: [To be provided at filing]
Email: issdandavis7795@gmail.com
Citizenship: United States
I hereby declare that I am the original and sole inventor of the subject matter described in this application.
Conclusion
This provisional application establishes priority for a novel context-and-time-bound post-quantum encryption system with demonstrated implementations and clear advantages over existing cryptographic schemes. The harmonic scaling law, temporal lattice stabilization, and dual-lattice architecture represent patentable innovations addressing critical vulnerabilities in current encryption systems.
Next Steps:
File this provisional by January 31, 2026 ($150 USPTO fee)
Continue development and testing through Q1-Q3 2026
Engage cryptographer for peer review (per vulnerability assessment)
File non-provisional with USPTO attorney by January 2027
Consider international filing (PCT) if commercial traction achieved
Document Status: Draft for review
Target Word Count: ~2,500 words (within provisional guidelines)
Attachments Needed:
GitHub repository link with implementation code
Test vectors and benchmarks
Inventor contact information
USPTO filing fee payment confirmation
